perturbation.original_text: In case of failing to fulfill the obligations of the SENDER as a result of any actions or illegal omissions or deviations from the Contract, the SENDER agrees to pay ECOPETROL as a penalty, an amount equivalent to ten percent (10%) of the final value of the Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: In case of failing to fulfill the obligations of the SENDER as a result of any actions or omissions or deviations from the Contract, the SENDER agrees to pay ECOPETROL as a penalty, an amount equivalent to fifty percent (50%) of the final value of the Contract.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the penalty to fifty percent of the contract's final value could be deemed an excessive penalty, potentially violating regulations against disproportionate penalties.
perturbation.law_explanation: U.C.C. § 2-718(1) states that damages for breach may be liquidated but only at an amount reasonable in light of the anticipated or actual harm. A 50% penalty might be viewed as unreasonably large and therefore unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-718. Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-718. Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits. (1) Damages for breach by either party may be liquidated in the agreement but only at an amount which is reasonable in the light of the anticipated or actual harm caused by the breach, the difficulties of proof of loss, and the inconvenience or nonfeasibility of otherwise obtaining an adequate remedy. A term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is void as a penalty. (2) Where the seller justifiably withholds delivery of goods because of the buyer's breach, the buyer is entitled to restitution of any amount by which the sum of his payments exceeds (a) the amount to which the seller is entitled by virtue of terms liquidating the seller's damages in accordance with subsection (1), or (b) in the absence of such terms, twenty per cent of the value of the total performance for which the buyer is obligated under the contract or $500, whichever is smaller. (3) The buyer's right to restitution under subsection (2) is subject to offset to the extent that the seller establishes (a) a right to recover damages under the provisions of this Article other than subsection (1), and (b) the amount or value of any benefits received by the buyer directly or indirectly by reason of the contract . (4) Where a seller has received payment in goods their reasonable value or the proceeds of their resale shall be treated as payments for the purposes of subsection (2); but if the seller has notice of the buyer's breach before reselling goods received in part performance, his resale is subject to the conditions laid down in this Article on resale by an aggrieved seller (Section 2-706 ). Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text increases the penalty for failing to fulfill obligations from 10% to 50% of the contract's final value. The Law Explanation suggests this could be viewed as unreasonably large. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically U.C.C. § 2-718(1), states that liquidated damages must be 'reasonable in light of the anticipated or actual harm caused by the breach' and that 'A term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is void as a penalty.' Increasing the penalty to 50% introduces a legal contradiction because it potentially violates the principle that liquidated damages must be reasonable and not punitive.

perturbation.original_text: The Transporter shall exercise custody on the Hydrocarbon from the time the Sender or whoever the Sender designates, delivers it at the Point of Entrance and until the Point of Exit.
perturbation.changed_text: The Transporter shall exercise custody on the Hydrocarbon from the time the Sender or whoever the Sender designates, declares intention to deliver at the Point of Entrance and until the Point of Exit.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'delivers it' to 'declares intention to deliver' alters when custody transfers, potentially conflicting with regulations regarding liability for loss or damage during transportation.
perturbation.law_explanation: 49 U.S. Code § 80113 outlines liability for carriers based on receipt of property for transportation. Changing the contract to indicate custody begins upon 'declaration of intention' may create a conflict as the actual delivery must have taken place. This contradicts federal regulations.

Liability for nonreceipt, misdescription, and improper loading (a) Liability for Nonreceipt and Misdescription .-Except as provided in this section, a common carrier issuing a bill of lading is liable for damages caused by nonreceipt by the carrier of any part of the goods by the date shown in the bill or by failure of the goods to correspond with the description contained in the bill. perturbation.justification: The modification from "delivers it" to "declares intention to deliver" fundamentally alters when the transporter's custody and liability begin. 49 U.S. Code § 80113 outlines liability for carriers based on the 'receipt of property for transportation.' The original text aligns with this, as custody transfers upon delivery. However, the changed text suggests custody begins merely with a declaration, before the carrier actually receives the hydrocarbon. This creates a contradiction because liability under 49 USC 80113 is triggered by 'nonreceipt...of any part of the goods,' which presumes actual transfer. The modification shifts the point of liability, potentially leaving a gap between the declaration and actual delivery, where responsibility is unclear and potentially contradicting federal regulations.
